name: Tadka Dal
preparation: Wash and pressure cook toor dal with turmeric and 2 cups water for 3-4 whistles until soft.
Mash the dal well and adjust consistency with water.
Heat ghee in a small pan for tadka. Add cumin seeds and let them splutter.
Add sliced garlic and dried red chillies, fry until garlic turns golden.
Add chopped onion and sauté until translucent.
Add chopped tomato, green chillies, and red chilli powder. Cook until tomato softens.
Pour the tadka over the cooked dal and mix well.
Simmer for 2-3 minutes. Garnish with fresh coriander and serve hot with rice or roti.
notes: A staple North Indian dal with a fragrant ghee tempering. Use less ghee for a lighter version.

name: Masoor Dal
preparation: Wash masoor dal thoroughly and soak for 10 minutes.
Pressure cook dal with turmeric and water for 2-3 whistles.
Heat oil in a pan, add cumin seeds and asafoetida.
Add chopped onion and sauté until golden brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste and green chillies, cook for a minute.
Add chopped tomato and cook until it turns mushy.
Add the cooked dal and mix well. Adjust consistency with water.
Add garam masala and salt, simmer for 5 minutes.
Garnish with fresh coriander and serve with steamed rice.
notes: Masoor dal cooks quickly and is high in protein. A wholesome everyday lunch dal.

name: Chana Dal
preparation: Wash and soak chana dal for 30 minutes.
Pressure cook with turmeric, bay leaf, cloves, and water for 4-5 whistles.
Heat oil in a pan, add cumin seeds and asafoetida.
Add chopped onion and cook until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and sauté for a minute.
Add tomato, coriander powder, and red chilli powder. Cook until tomato is soft.
Add the cooked chana dal and mix well.
Simmer for 5-7 minutes until flavors meld. Adjust salt and consistency.
Garnish with coriander leaves and serve with rice or roti.
notes: Chana dal has a nutty flavor and firm texture. Rich in fiber and plant protein.

name: Moong Dal Fry
preparation: Wash moong dal and pressure cook with turmeric and water for 3 whistles.
Mash the dal lightly with a ladle.
Heat ghee in a pan, add mustard seeds and cumin seeds.
When they splutter, add curry leaves and asafoetida.
Add chopped onion and sauté until light golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and green chillies, cook for a minute.
Add tomato and cook until soft and mushy.
Pour in the cooked dal, add salt, and simmer for 5 minutes.
Squeeze lemon juice, garnish with coriander, and serve hot with rice.
notes: Light and easy to digest. Moong dal fry is perfect for everyday lunch, gentle on the stomach.

name: Toor Dal
preparation: Rinse toor dal and pressure cook with turmeric and water for 3-4 whistles.
Mash the dal to a smooth consistency.
Heat oil in a tadka pan, add mustard seeds and cumin seeds.
When they splutter, add curry leaves, asafoetida, and green chillies.
Add chopped onion and cook until soft.
Add tomato and red chilli powder, cook until tomato breaks down.
Pour this tempering into the cooked dal and mix well.
Simmer for 3-4 minutes, adjust salt and consistency.
Garnish with coriander and serve with steamed rice.
notes: The most commonly used dal in South Indian cooking. Pairs perfectly with plain rice and papad.

name: Dal Makhani Light
preparation: Soak urad dal and rajma overnight or for 6-8 hours.
Pressure cook with turmeric, bay leaf, cardamom, and water for 6-7 whistles until very soft.
Mash partially with a ladle.
Heat butter in a heavy-bottomed pan, add cumin seeds.
Add chopped onion and sauté until deep golden brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for a minute.
Add tomato puree, red chilli powder, and cook until oil separates.
Add the cooked dal and mix well. Simmer on low heat for 15-20 minutes.
Stir in low-fat cream and garam masala. Cook for 5 more minutes.
Serve hot with rice or naan.
notes: Lighter version of the classic Punjabi dal makhani using less butter and low-fat cream. Still rich and creamy.

name: Dal Palak
ingredients: 1 cup toor dal or moong dal
preparation: Wash and pressure cook dal with turmeric and water for 3-4 whistles.
Blanch spinach in hot water for 2 minutes, drain and roughly chop.
Heat ghee in a pan, add cumin seeds and asafoetida.
Add chopped garlic and green chillies, sauté until fragrant.
Add onion and cook until soft.
Add tomato and cook until mushy.
Add blanched spinach and cook for 2-3 minutes.
Add the cooked dal, garam masala, and salt. Mix well.
Simmer for 5-7 minutes. Serve hot with roti or rice.
notes: A nutritious combination of lentils and spinach. Rich in iron and protein, perfect for a healthy lunch.

name: Dal Lasooni
preparation: Wash and pressure cook toor dal with turmeric and water for 3-4 whistles.
Mash the dal well.
Heat ghee in a pan, add cumin seeds and dried red chillies.
Add finely chopped garlic and fry until golden and aromatic.
Add asafoetida and chopped onion, sauté until golden.
Add tomato and red chilli powder, cook until soft.
Pour this garlic tempering over the dal and mix.
Simmer for 5 minutes until flavors meld.
Garnish with coriander leaves and serve with rice or roti.
notes: Lasooni means garlic. This dal has a bold garlic flavor that adds warmth and helps boost immunity.

name: Panchmel Dal
preparation: Wash all five dals together and soak for 20 minutes.
Pressure cook with turmeric and water for 4-5 whistles until soft.
Mash lightly with a ladle.
Heat ghee in a pan, add cumin seeds and asafoetida.
Add chopped onion and cook until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and green chillies, sauté briefly.
Add tomato, red chilli powder, and coriander powder. Cook until soft.
Pour the tempering into the dal and simmer for 5-7 minutes.
Garnish with coriander and serve with bajra roti or rice.
notes: A Rajasthani specialty made with five lentils. Combines the nutrition and flavors of multiple dals.

name: Sambhar
ingredients: 1/2 cup mixed vegetables (drumstick, carrot, brinjal, pumpkin)
preparation: Cook toor dal with turmeric and water in a pressure cooker for 3-4 whistles. Mash well.
Extract tamarind juice by soaking in warm water and squeezing out the pulp.
Boil mixed vegetables in water until tender.
Heat oil in a pan, add mustard seeds and fenugreek seeds.
When they splutter, add dried red chillies, curry leaves, and asafoetida.
Add chopped onion and sauté until soft.
Add tomato and cook until mushy.
Add sambhar powder and cook for a minute.
Add cooked vegetables, tamarind juice, and the mashed dal.
Add salt and simmer for 10-12 minutes on low heat.
Garnish with coriander and serve with rice, idli, or dosa.
notes: The quintessential South Indian lentil stew. Use fresh drumstick and seasonal vegetables for best flavor.

name: Rajma Masala
preparation: Soak rajma overnight in plenty of water.
Pressure cook rajma with bay leaves, cardamom, cinnamon, and water for 7-8 whistles until very soft.
Heat oil in a heavy pan, add cumin seeds.
Add chopped onions and cook until deep golden brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste and sauté for 2 minutes.
Add tomato puree, turmeric, red chilli powder, and coriander powder.
Cook until oil separates from the masala.
Add the cooked rajma with its liquid and mix well.
Simmer on low heat for 15-20 minutes until gravy thickens.
Add garam masala, garnish with coriander, and serve with steamed rice.
notes: Classic Punjabi rajma chawal combination. Ensure rajma is well-cooked as undercooked kidney beans can be harmful.

name: Chole Masala
preparation: Soak chickpeas overnight. Boil with a tea bag to get dark color, then discard tea bag.
Pressure cook chickpeas with bay leaf, black cardamom, cinnamon, and water for 6-7 whistles.
Heat oil in a pan, add cumin seeds.
Add chopped onions and cook until deep brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for a minute.
Add tomato puree, turmeric, red chilli powder, and chole masala powder.
Cook masala until oil separates.
Add cooked chickpeas with some liquid and mix well.
Simmer for 15-20 minutes on low heat. Crush a few chickpeas to thicken the gravy.
Garnish with coriander and serve with bhature, rice, or roti.
notes: Iconic Punjabi street food when paired with bhature. The tea bag trick gives authentic dark color.

name: Kala Chana Curry
ingredients: 1 cup kala chana (black chickpeas) soaked overnight
preparation: Soak kala chana overnight in water.
Pressure cook with turmeric and water for 7-8 whistles until tender.
Heat oil in a pan, add cumin seeds.
Add chopped onion and cook until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and sauté for a minute.
Add tomato, coriander powder, and red chilli powder. Cook until tomato is soft.
Add cooked kala chana with some liquid.
Simmer for 10-12 minutes until gravy thickens.
Add amchur and garam masala. Garnish with coriander.
Serve hot with poori, roti, or rice.
notes: Kala chana is higher in fiber than white chickpeas. A nutritious and filling curry.

name: Lobhia Curry
preparation: Soak lobhia for 4-6 hours and drain.
Pressure cook with turmeric and water for 4-5 whistles until soft.
Heat oil in a pan, add cumin seeds.
Add chopped onion and sauté until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for a minute.
Add tomato, coriander powder, and red chilli powder. Cook until tomato breaks down.
Add cooked lobhia with some cooking liquid.
Simmer for 8-10 minutes until gravy thickens.
Add garam masala, garnish with coriander.
Serve with steamed rice or roti.
notes: Black-eyed peas are easy to digest and cook faster than other beans. Good source of folate.

name: Chana Palak
preparation: Soak chickpeas overnight and pressure cook with turmeric and water for 6-7 whistles.
Blanch spinach in boiling water for 2 minutes, then blend to a coarse paste.
Heat oil in a pan, add cumin seeds.
Add onion and sauté until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste, coriander powder, and red chilli powder.
Add tomato and cook until soft.
Add the spinach paste and cook for 3-4 minutes.
Add cooked chickpeas and mix well.
Simmer for 8-10 minutes. Add garam masala and salt.
Serve hot with roti or rice.
notes: A protein and iron-rich combination of chickpeas and spinach. Great for a nutritious lunch.

name: Aloo Gobi
preparation: Wash and cut potatoes into cubes and cauliflower into small florets.
Heat oil in a pan, add cumin seeds.
Add chopped onion and cook until soft.
Add ginger-garlic paste and green chillies, sauté briefly.
Add tomato, turmeric, coriander powder, and red chilli powder. Cook until tomato softens.
Add potato cubes first, stir well, cover and cook for 5 minutes.
Add cauliflower florets, sprinkle salt, mix gently.
Cover and cook on low heat for 12-15 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Add garam masala, garnish with coriander.
Serve hot with roti or paratha.
notes: A beloved everyday sabzi found in every Indian household. Cook on low heat for best results.

name: Bhindi Masala
preparation: Wash bhindi and pat completely dry with a towel. Cut into 1-inch pieces.
Heat oil in a wide pan, add cumin seeds.
Add sliced onion and sauté until light golden.
Add bhindi pieces and spread in a single layer. Cook on medium heat without stirring for 3-4 minutes.
Stir gently, add turmeric and salt. Cook uncovered for 8-10 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Add chopped tomato, coriander powder, red chilli powder, and green chillies.
Cook for another 5 minutes until bhindi is crisp and not slimy.
Add amchur and garam masala. Toss well.
Serve hot with roti or paratha.
notes: Key tip: bhindi must be completely dry before cutting to avoid sliminess. Cook uncovered on medium heat.

name: Baingan Bharta
ingredients: 2 tbsp mustard oil or regular oil
preparation: Roast the whole eggplant directly on a gas flame, turning regularly, until skin is charred and flesh is soft (15-20 minutes).
Let it cool slightly, peel off the charred skin, and mash the flesh. Keep it chunky.
Heat oil in a pan, add cumin seeds.
Add chopped onion and sauté until golden brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste and green chillies, cook for a minute.
Add tomatoes, turmeric, and red chilli powder. Cook until tomatoes are soft and mushy.
Add the mashed roasted eggplant and mix well.
Cook on medium heat for 7-8 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Add garam masala and salt. Garnish with coriander.
Serve hot with roti or paratha.
notes: The smoky flavor from roasting on open flame is what makes authentic bharta. Use mustard oil for traditional Punjabi taste.

name: Lauki Kofta Light
preparation: Grate lauki and squeeze out excess water. Mix with besan, garam masala, and salt.
Shape into small balls and shallow fry until golden on all sides. Drain on paper towel.
For gravy: Heat oil, add onion puree and cook until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for a minute.
Add tomato puree, turmeric, coriander powder, and red chilli powder.
Cook until oil separates from the masala.
Add 1 cup water and bring to a simmer.
Whisk yogurt and add to the gravy slowly, stirring continuously.
Gently add the koftas to the gravy just before serving.
Garnish with coriander and serve with roti or rice.
notes: Light version uses shallow frying instead of deep frying. Add koftas to gravy at serving time to prevent them from getting soggy.

name: Gobhi Masala
preparation: Cut cauliflower into medium-sized florets and wash well.
Heat oil in a pan, add cumin seeds.
Add chopped onion and sauté until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and green chillies, cook briefly.
Add tomato, turmeric, coriander powder, and red chilli powder. Cook until tomato softens.
Add cauliflower florets, mix well to coat with masala.
Add salt, cover and cook on low heat for 12-15 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Cook until cauliflower is tender but not mushy.
Add garam masala, garnish with coriander.
Serve with roti or paratha.
notes: A simple yet flavorful cauliflower curry. Don't overcook - cauliflower should hold its shape.

name: Palak Paneer
preparation: Blanch spinach in boiling water for 2 minutes, then transfer to ice water.
Drain and blend spinach into a smooth puree.
Cut paneer into cubes.
Heat butter in a pan, add cumin seeds.
Add chopped onion and cook until soft.
Add ginger-garlic paste and green chillies, sauté for a minute.
Add tomato and turmeric, cook until tomato softens.
Add the spinach puree and cook for 5 minutes on medium heat.
Add paneer cubes, cream, garam masala, and salt.
Simmer for 3-4 minutes. Serve hot with roti or naan.
notes: Blanching spinach in ice water retains the bright green color. A protein-rich, iron-rich North Indian favorite.

name: Matar Paneer
preparation: Cut paneer into cubes. Boil peas until tender if using fresh peas.
Heat oil in a pan, add cumin seeds.
Add chopped onion and cook until golden brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste and sauté for a minute.
Add tomato puree, turmeric, coriander powder, and red chilli powder.
Cook until oil separates from the masala.
Add 1/2 cup water and bring to a simmer.
Add green peas and cook for 3-4 minutes.
Add paneer cubes and garam masala. Simmer for 5 minutes.
Add cream if using, garnish with coriander, and serve with roti or rice.
notes: A classic North Indian paneer curry with sweet green peas. Use fresh peas in season for best taste.

name: Paneer Butter Masala Light
preparation: Sauté onions and tomatoes until soft. Add soaked cashews and blend to a smooth paste.
Heat butter in a pan, add cumin seeds.
Add ginger-garlic paste and sauté for a minute.
Add the onion-tomato-cashew paste and cook for 5-7 minutes.
Add turmeric, red chilli powder, coriander powder, and salt.
Cook until the masala thickens and oil starts separating.
Add 1/2 cup water to adjust consistency.
Add paneer cubes and simmer for 5 minutes.
Add low-fat cream, garam masala, and crushed kasuri methi.
Cook for 2 more minutes and serve hot with naan or roti.
notes: Light version uses less butter and low-fat cream. Kasuri methi adds the signature restaurant-style flavor.

name: Kadai Paneer
preparation: Dry roast coriander seeds and dried red chillies, then coarsely grind them. This is the kadai masala.
Heat oil in a kadai or wok.
Add ginger-garlic paste and sauté for a minute.
Add tomatoes and cook until soft and pulpy.
Add the ground kadai masala and turmeric. Cook for 2-3 minutes.
Add onion chunks and capsicum chunks. Stir-fry for 3-4 minutes, keeping them crunchy.
Add paneer cubes and toss gently.
Add salt, garam masala, and crushed kasuri methi.
Cook for 2-3 minutes and garnish with coriander.
Serve hot with tandoori roti or naan.
notes: The freshly ground kadai masala of coriander seeds and red chillies gives this dish its signature flavor.

name: Shahi Paneer Light
ingredients: 4-5 saffron strands soaked in warm milk
preparation: Soak cashews in warm water for 15 minutes and blend to a paste.
Sauté onions until golden, add tomatoes and cook until soft. Blend to a puree.
Heat oil in a pan, add green cardamom.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for a minute.
Add the onion-tomato puree, turmeric, and red chilli powder. Cook for 5 minutes.
Add cashew paste and cook for 3-4 minutes.
Add 1/2 cup water for desired consistency.
Add paneer cubes and saffron milk. Simmer for 5 minutes.
Add cream and garam masala. Cook for 2 more minutes.
Garnish with coriander and serve with naan.
notes: Shahi means royal - saffron and cashew paste make this rich. Light version uses less cream and oil.

name: Paneer Tikka Masala
preparation: Marinate paneer, capsicum, and onion cubes in yogurt, spices, and lemon juice for 30 minutes.
Grill or pan-fry the marinated pieces until charred on edges.
For gravy: Heat butter, add ginger-garlic paste and cook briefly.
Add onion puree and sauté until golden.
Add tomato puree and cook until oil separates.
Add red chilli powder, salt, and 1/2 cup water.
Simmer for 5-7 minutes until gravy thickens.
Add the grilled paneer tikka pieces to the gravy.
Add cream, garam masala, and kasuri methi.
Simmer for 3 minutes and serve with naan or tandoori roti.
notes: A restaurant favorite! Grilling the paneer tikka first gives a smoky flavor to the creamy masala gravy.

name: Paneer Bhurji Gravy
preparation: Crumble the paneer into small pieces by hand.
Heat oil in a pan, add cumin seeds.
Add chopped onions and cook until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and green chillies, sauté briefly.
Add capsicum and cook for 2 minutes.
Add tomatoes, turmeric, coriander powder, and red chilli powder.
Cook until tomatoes are soft and masala is well cooked.
Add crumbled paneer and 1/2 cup water for gravy consistency.
Simmer for 5-7 minutes, add garam masala and salt.
Garnish with coriander and serve with roti or paratha.
notes: Gravy version of the popular paneer bhurji. Quick to make and protein-rich for a satisfying lunch.

name: Paneer Do Pyaza
preparation: Cut paneer into cubes. Slice 2 onions and quarter the third.
Heat oil in a pan, add cumin seeds.
Add sliced onions and cook until deep golden brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste and sauté for a minute.
Add tomato puree, turmeric, coriander powder, and red chilli powder.
Cook until masala thickens and oil separates.
Add 1/2 cup water for gravy consistency.
Add paneer cubes and quartered onion. Simmer for 5 minutes.
The quartered onion should remain slightly crunchy.
Add garam masala, kasuri methi, and coriander. Serve with roti.
notes: Do Pyaza means double onions - cooked and raw onion chunks give two textures. A Mughlai-style dish.

name: Malai Paneer Light
preparation: Soak cashews and blend with milk to a smooth paste.
Heat butter, add cardamom and peppercorns.
Add onion puree and sauté until light golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for a minute.
Add cashew-milk paste and cook on low heat for 5 minutes.
Add turmeric and white pepper powder.
Add paneer cubes and simmer for 5 minutes.
Add low-fat cream and sugar. Stir gently.
Cook for 3-4 more minutes on low heat.
Serve hot with naan or pulao. Garnish with cream drizzle.
notes: A mild, creamy white gravy curry. Perfect for those who prefer less spicy food. Light version uses low-fat dairy.

name: Paneer Jalfrezi
preparation: Cut all vegetables into thick strips or chunks.
Heat oil in a wok or pan on high heat.
Add ginger-garlic paste and green chillies, sauté briefly.
Add onion chunks and stir-fry for 2 minutes on high heat.
Add carrot and capsicum strips, stir-fry for 2-3 minutes keeping them crunchy.
Add tomato wedges, turmeric, coriander powder, and red chilli powder.
Add soy sauce and vinegar, toss well.
Add paneer cubes and toss gently.
Add garam masala and salt. Cook for 2 minutes.
Serve hot with roti or fried rice.
notes: Jalfrezi is a stir-fry style dish with crunchy vegetables. Cook on high heat and don't overcook the veggies.

name: Veg Biryani
ingredients: 1 cup mixed vegetables (carrot, beans, peas, cauliflower)
A few saffron strands in warm milk
preparation: Soak basmati rice for 30 minutes. Parboil rice with whole spices until 70% cooked, drain.
Heat oil in a heavy-bottomed pot, add sliced onion and fry until deep golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and green chillies, cook briefly.
Add mixed vegetables and sauté for 3-4 minutes.
Add tomato, biryani masala, turmeric, and yogurt. Cook for 5 minutes.
Layer the parboiled rice over the vegetable masala.
Sprinkle saffron milk, mint, and coriander on top.
Cover tightly with a lid and cook on very low heat (dum) for 20 minutes.
Let it rest for 5 minutes, then gently mix from bottom.
Serve with raita and salan.
notes: Dum cooking is essential for authentic biryani flavor. Don't open the lid during cooking for best results.

name: Paneer Biryani
preparation: Soak rice for 30 minutes and parboil until 70% done with whole spices.
Lightly pan-fry paneer cubes until golden on all sides. Set aside.
Heat ghee, fry sliced onions until crispy and golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for a minute.
Add tomato, biryani masala, turmeric, and yogurt. Cook for 5 minutes.
Add paneer cubes and mix gently.
In a heavy pot, layer the paneer masala at the bottom.
Layer parboiled rice on top.
Drizzle saffron milk, add mint and coriander leaves.
Seal with lid and cook on dum (very low heat) for 20 minutes. Serve with raita.
notes: Pan-frying paneer before layering gives it a golden crust that holds up well during dum cooking.

name: Hyderabadi Veg Biryani
ingredients: 1 cup mixed vegetables (carrot, beans, potato, peas)
preparation: Soak rice and parboil with whole spices until 70% cooked. Drain.
Deep fry sliced onions until crispy golden brown. Set aside.
Cook mixed vegetables until half done.
Heat ghee, add ginger-garlic paste and green chillies.
Add tomatoes, biryani masala, turmeric, and whisked yogurt.
Cook masala for 7-8 minutes until oil separates.
Add half-cooked vegetables and mix well.
In a thick-bottomed pot, layer: masala, rice, saffron milk, fried onions, mint, coriander.
Repeat layers. Seal pot with dough or tight lid.
Cook on high heat for 3 minutes, then very low heat for 25 minutes. Rest 5 minutes before opening.
notes: Hyderabadi-style uses the dum pukht technique. Sealing with dough traps steam for perfect biryani.

name: Lucknowi Biryani Light
preparation: Soak rice for 30 minutes and parboil with whole spices until 70% cooked.
Soak saffron in warm milk.
Heat ghee, fry sliced onion until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste, cook for a minute.
Add yogurt, biryani masala, nutmeg, and mace. Cook for 5 minutes.
Add vegetables and cook for 3 minutes.
Layer rice over the masala in a heavy pot.
Drizzle saffron milk and rose water over the rice.
Top with mint leaves. Seal tightly.
Cook on very low heat for 20 minutes. Rest 5 minutes. Serve with korma.
notes: Lucknowi (Awadhi) biryani is milder and more aromatic than Hyderabadi, using rose water and kewra.

name: Tawa Pulao
preparation: Cook rice and let it cool. Grains should be separate.
Heat oil and butter on a tawa or large flat pan.
Add chopped onion and sauté until soft.
Add green chillies and mixed vegetables. Cook for 3-4 minutes.
Add chopped tomato and cook until soft.
Add pav bhaji masala, turmeric, red chilli powder, and salt.
Mix the masala well and cook for 2 minutes.
Add the cooked rice and toss gently on medium heat.
Squeeze lemon juice and mix everything well.
Garnish with coriander and serve hot with raita.
notes: A popular Mumbai street food made on a flat tawa. Pav bhaji masala is the secret ingredient for the iconic taste.

name: Jeera Rice With Dal
preparation: Soak rice for 20-30 minutes.
Heat ghee in a pan, add cumin seeds, bay leaf, and cloves.
When cumin splutters, add soaked and drained rice.
Sauté for 2 minutes, add 2 cups water and salt.
Cook on low heat until water is absorbed and rice is fluffy.
For dal: Pressure cook toor dal with turmeric for 3 whistles.
Heat ghee, add cumin seeds, garlic, red chilli, and asafoetida.
Pour tadka over dal, add salt.
Serve jeera rice with a bowl of hot tadka dal on the side.
notes: The simplest and most comforting Indian lunch. Aromatic jeera rice pairs perfectly with hot dal.

name: Lemon Rice
preparation: Cook rice and spread on a plate to cool. Grains should be separate.
Heat oil in a pan, add mustard seeds.
When they splutter, add urad dal and chana dal. Fry until golden.
Add peanuts and fry until they turn light brown.
Add curry leaves, dried red chillies, green chillies, and asafoetida.
Add turmeric powder and mix well.
Add the cooled rice and toss gently to coat with the tempering.
Add lemon juice and salt, mix carefully without breaking the rice.
Garnish with coriander and serve with papad or pickle.
notes: A tangy South Indian rice dish. Always add lemon juice off the heat or at the end to preserve its fresh flavor.

name: Coconut Rice
preparation: Cook rice and let it cool. Fluff with a fork.
Heat coconut oil in a pan, add mustard seeds.
When they splutter, add urad dal and chana dal. Fry until golden.
Add cashew nuts and fry until light golden.
Add dried red chillies, green chillies, curry leaves, and asafoetida.
Add fresh grated coconut and sauté for 2-3 minutes on low heat.
Add the cooked rice, salt, and toss gently.
Mix well until rice is evenly coated with coconut.
Serve with any curry, pickle, or papad.
notes: Use freshly grated coconut for the best flavor. Coconut oil enhances the aroma of this South Indian staple.

name: Curd Rice
preparation: Cook rice until slightly soft and mushy. Let it cool.
Mash the rice lightly with a spoon.
Add yogurt and milk, mix well. The consistency should be creamy.
Heat oil in a small pan, add mustard seeds.
When they splutter, add urad dal and fry until golden.
Add curry leaves, green chillies, grated ginger, and asafoetida.
Pour this tempering over the curd rice and mix.
Add salt and mix gently. Add grated carrot and pomegranate if using.
Refrigerate for 30 minutes for best taste.
Serve cold with pickle as a soothing lunch.
notes: A cooling and probiotic-rich South Indian comfort food. Perfect for hot summer days. Best served chilled.

name: Tomato Rice
preparation: Wash and soak rice for 20 minutes.
Heat oil, add mustard seeds and cumin seeds.
When they splutter, add urad dal, peanuts, and fry until golden.
Add curry leaves, green chillies, and asafoetida.
Add chopped onion and sauté until soft.
Add tomato puree, turmeric, and red chilli powder. Cook for 5 minutes.
Add soaked rice and 2 cups water. Add salt.
Bring to a boil, then cook on low heat until rice is done (15-18 minutes).
Fluff with a fork and garnish with coriander.
Serve with raita or papad.
notes: A one-pot tangy rice dish from South India. Use ripe red tomatoes for the best color and flavor.

name: Tamarind Rice
preparation: Soak tamarind in warm water for 15 minutes. Extract thick tamarind juice.
Heat sesame oil, add mustard seeds and fenugreek seeds.
When they splutter, add urad dal, chana dal, and peanuts. Fry until golden.
Add dried red chillies, curry leaves, and asafoetida.
Add turmeric and the tamarind juice. Add salt and jaggery if using.
Cook the tamarind paste on medium heat for 8-10 minutes until it thickens and oil separates.
Let the tamarind paste cool slightly.
Add cooled cooked rice and mix gently until well coated.
Let it sit for 10-15 minutes for flavors to absorb.
Serve with papad or appalam.
notes: Also called Puliyodarai in Tamil Nadu. The tamarind paste can be made ahead and stored for a week.

name: Bisi Bele Bath
ingredients: 1/2 cup mixed vegetables (carrot, beans, peas, potato)
preparation: Cook rice and toor dal together in a pressure cooker with turmeric for 4-5 whistles.
The rice-dal should be soft and mushy.
Cook mixed vegetables separately until tender.
Extract tamarind juice from the soaked tamarind.
Add tamarind juice, cooked vegetables, and bisi bele bath powder to the rice-dal.
Add salt and mix well. Simmer on low heat for 10 minutes.
Heat ghee, add mustard seeds, curry leaves, dried red chillies, cashew nuts, and asafoetida.
Pour this tempering over the bisi bele bath.
Mix well and adjust consistency (should be porridge-like).
Garnish with coriander and serve hot with boondi raita and papad.
notes: A Karnataka one-pot meal meaning 'hot lentil rice'. The special bisi bele bath powder is key to its unique flavor.

name: Vangi Bath
preparation: Cook rice and cool. Extract tamarind juice.
Heat oil, add mustard seeds, urad dal, chana dal, and peanuts.
When golden, add curry leaves and asafoetida.
Add chopped onion and sauté until soft.
Add cubed brinjal, turmeric, and salt. Cover and cook for 8-10 minutes until brinjal is soft.
Add vangi bath powder and tamarind juice.
Cook for 5 minutes until masala thickens.
Add cooked rice and mix gently until well coated.
Cook for 2-3 minutes. Let rest for 5 minutes.
Serve hot with papad and boondi raita.
notes: A Karnataka specialty featuring brinjal (vangi). The vangi bath powder blend of spices gives it a unique taste.

name: Puliyodarai
preparation: Soak tamarind in warm water and extract thick juice.
Heat gingelly oil, add mustard seeds and fenugreek seeds.
When they splutter, add urad dal, chana dal, and peanuts. Fry until golden.
Add dried red chillies, curry leaves, and asafoetida.
Add turmeric powder and stir.
Pour in the tamarind extract, add salt and jaggery.
Cook on medium heat for 10-12 minutes until the paste thickens and becomes glossy.
Remove from heat and let the tamarind paste cool.
Mix with cooled rice, ensuring every grain is coated.
Serve at room temperature with appalam.
notes: Tamil Nadu temple-style tamarind rice. Gingelly oil is traditional and essential. Tastes better after resting.

name: Bagara Rice
preparation: Wash and soak basmati rice for 20-30 minutes.
Heat oil in a heavy-bottomed pan, add cumin seeds, bay leaf, cloves, cardamom, and cinnamon.
Add cashew nuts and fry until golden.
Add sliced onion and sauté until golden brown.
Add green chillies, curry leaves, and turmeric powder.
Add drained rice and sauté gently for 2 minutes.
Add 2 cups water and salt. Bring to a boil.
Reduce heat to low, cover tightly, and cook for 15 minutes.
Let it rest for 5 minutes. Fluff with a fork.
Garnish with mint leaves. Serve with Hyderabadi salan or raita.
notes: Hyderabadi spiced rice, often served alongside biryani or as a standalone with salan. The whole spices are aromatic.

name: Khichdi Moong
preparation: Wash rice and moong dal together until water runs clear.
Heat ghee in a pressure cooker, add cumin seeds and asafoetida.
Add washed rice and dal, sauté for a minute.
Add turmeric, salt, and 2 cups water.
Pressure cook for 3-4 whistles until soft and mushy.
Open the cooker and stir to a porridge-like consistency.
Add more hot water if it's too thick.
Serve hot with a dollop of ghee on top.
Accompany with pickle, papad, and yogurt.
notes: The ultimate Indian comfort food. Easy to digest and often served when feeling unwell. Simple yet satisfying.

name: Masala Khichdi
preparation: Wash rice and dal together.
Heat ghee in a pressure cooker, add cumin seeds.
Add chopped onion and cook until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and green chillies, sauté briefly.
Add chopped tomato and cook until soft.
Add turmeric, red chilli powder, and vegetables. Stir for 2 minutes.
Add washed rice and dal, sauté for a minute.
Add water and salt. Pressure cook for 3 whistles.
Let pressure release naturally. Stir well.
Garnish with coriander. Serve with kadhi, raita, or pickle.
notes: A more flavorful version of plain khichdi with vegetables and spices. One-pot meal that's nutritious and filling.

name: Sabudana Khichdi Lunch
preparation: Soak sabudana in just enough water to cover for 6-8 hours or overnight. Drain completely.
Heat oil in a non-stick pan, add cumin seeds.
When they splutter, add curry leaves and green chillies.
Add cubed potatoes and cook on medium heat for 8-10 minutes until tender.
Add soaked and drained sabudana, salt, and sugar.
Mix gently and cook on medium-low heat for 5-7 minutes.
Stir occasionally to prevent sticking. Sabudana will become translucent when cooked.
Add crushed peanuts and toss gently.
Squeeze lemon juice and garnish with coriander.
Serve hot as a lunch meal.
notes: Though popular during fasting, this makes a filling lunch too. Key: soak sabudana well and cook on medium heat to avoid sticking.

name: Bajra Khichdi
ingredients: 1 cup bajra (pearl millet) coarsely ground or whole
preparation: Wash bajra and moong dal together.
Heat ghee in a pressure cooker, add cumin seeds and asafoetida.
Add washed bajra and dal, sauté for a minute.
Add vegetables, turmeric, salt, and water.
Pressure cook for 4-5 whistles until soft.
Open and mash lightly. Adjust consistency with hot water.
The khichdi should be thick and porridge-like.
Top with a generous dollop of ghee.
Serve hot with buttermilk and pickle.
notes: A Rajasthani winter staple. Bajra is rich in iron and keeps the body warm. Traditionally served with a lot of ghee.

name: Dal Khichdi
preparation: Wash rice, moong dal, and masoor dal together.
Heat ghee in a pressure cooker, add cumin seeds.
Add asafoetida, curry leaves, green chillies, and grated ginger.
Add onion if using and sauté until soft.
Add the washed rice-dal mixture and sauté for a minute.
Add turmeric, salt, and water.
Pressure cook for 3-4 whistles.
Let pressure release naturally. Stir well.
Adjust consistency - it should be creamy and thick.
Serve hot with ghee, pickle, and papad.
notes: Two-dal khichdi is richer in protein than single-dal versions. A wholesome one-pot lunch meal.

name: Roti Sabzi Combo
ingredients: For sabzi: 2 cups mixed vegetables (potato, cauliflower, peas, beans)
preparation: Knead whole wheat flour with water and salt into a soft dough. Rest for 15 minutes.
For sabzi: Heat oil, add cumin seeds.
Add onion and sauté until golden.
Add tomato, turmeric, coriander powder, and red chilli powder. Cook until soft.
Add mixed vegetables, salt, and 1/4 cup water. Cover and cook for 12-15 minutes.
Add garam masala when vegetables are tender.
Divide dough into balls, roll into thin rotis.
Cook each roti on a hot tawa, pressing with a cloth to puff up.
Serve 3-4 hot rotis with the mixed vegetable sabzi.
notes: The most basic and balanced Indian lunch. Whole wheat roti provides complex carbs and fiber.

name: Chapati Curry Plate
ingredients: For curry: 1 cup paneer or mixed veg
preparation: Knead flour with water and salt into smooth dough. Rest covered for 20 minutes.
For curry: Heat oil, add cumin seeds.
Add onion and cook until golden brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste and sauté for a minute.
Add tomato puree with all spice powders and cook until oil separates.
Add paneer cubes or vegetables and 1/2 cup water.
Simmer for 8-10 minutes until gravy thickens.
Add garam masala and fresh coriander.
Roll dough into thin chapatis and cook on hot tawa.
Serve 3 chapatis with a generous bowl of curry.
notes: A complete and balanced lunch plate. Chapatis are thinner than rotis and cook quickly on a hot tawa.

name: Phulka Dal Plate
preparation: Knead flour with water and salt. Rest for 15 minutes.
Cook toor dal with turmeric and water in pressure cooker for 3-4 whistles.
Heat ghee, add cumin seeds, garlic, dried red chillies, and asafoetida.
Add onion and tomato, cook until soft.
Pour tadka into dal, add salt, and simmer 5 minutes.
Divide dough into small balls. Roll thin rounds.
Cook phulkas on hot tawa, then hold briefly over open flame to puff up.
Phulkas should be soft and puffed.
Serve 4 hot phulkas with a bowl of dal.
Add a side of pickle or salad for a complete meal.
notes: Phulkas are lighter than rotis since they puff on direct flame without oil. A healthy everyday lunch option.

name: Bajra Roti Garlic Chutney
preparation: Knead bajra flour with warm water and salt into a soft dough.
Dry roast garlic, red chillies, peanuts, and sesame seeds.
Grind them coarsely into garlic chutney with salt.
Divide bajra dough into balls. Pat flat between oiled palms or on a plastic sheet.
Cook bajra roti on a hot tawa on both sides until brown spots appear.
Apply ghee on the hot roti.
Serve 3 bajra rotis with garlic chutney and a bowl of yogurt.
You can also serve with any dal or sabzi on the side.
notes: A Rajasthani and Maharashtrian staple. Bajra is gluten-free and rich in iron. Best eaten fresh off the tawa.

name: Mix Veg Curry
ingredients: 1 cup mixed vegetables (carrot, beans, peas, cauliflower, potato)
preparation: Cut all vegetables into uniform pieces.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds.
Add onion and sauté until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook briefly.
Add tomato puree, turmeric, coriander powder, and red chilli powder.
Cook until masala thickens and oil separates.
Add cut vegetables and 1/2 cup water.
Cover and cook on medium heat for 12-15 minutes until vegetables are tender.
Add garam masala and salt. Garnish with coriander.
Serve with roti or rice.
notes: A versatile curry that uses whatever vegetables are in season. Great way to eat multiple vegetables in one dish.

name: Navratan Korma
ingredients: 1/4 cup mixed vegetables (carrot, beans, peas, cauliflower)
preparation: Soak cashews and blend with a little water to a paste.
Heat ghee, add bay leaf and cardamom.
Add sliced onion and sauté until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook briefly.
Add yogurt and all spice powders. Cook for 3-4 minutes.
Add cashew paste and 1/2 cup water. Simmer for 5 minutes.
Add mixed vegetables and cook for 8-10 minutes until tender.
Add paneer cubes and pineapple.
Add cream, garam masala, and saffron. Simmer 3 minutes.
Serve with naan or jeera rice.
notes: Navratan means nine gems - traditionally uses nine different ingredients. A rich Mughlai dish.

name: Veg Kolhapuri
ingredients: 1.5 cups mixed vegetables (potato, cauliflower, beans, carrot, peas)
2 tbsp kolhapuri masala or: 1 tbsp desiccated coconut, 4-5 dried red chillies, 1 tsp coriander seeds, 1/2 tsp cumin seeds, 4-5 peppercorns
preparation: Dry roast coconut, red chillies, coriander seeds, cumin seeds, and peppercorns. Grind to a fine paste with water.
Cut vegetables into pieces and boil until half-cooked.
Heat oil, add onions and sauté until brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for a minute.
Add chopped tomatoes and cook until soft.
Add the ground Kolhapuri masala paste and cook for 5 minutes.
Add the half-cooked vegetables and 1/2 cup water.
Simmer for 10-12 minutes until vegetables are tender and gravy thickens.
Garnish with coriander. Serve with bhakri or chapati.
notes: A fiery Maharashtrian curry from Kolhapur. Adjust red chillies to your spice tolerance.

name: Veg Jalfrezi
ingredients: 1 cup mixed vegetables (capsicum, onion, carrot, beans, baby corn)
preparation: Cut all vegetables into thick strips or chunks.
Heat oil on high heat in a wok.
Add ginger-garlic paste and green chillies, stir-fry briefly.
Add onion chunks and stir-fry for 2 minutes.
Add all vegetables and stir-fry on high heat for 3-4 minutes.
Add turmeric, coriander powder, and red chilli powder.
Add tomato wedges, soy sauce, and vinegar.
Toss everything together for 2-3 minutes.
Add garam masala and salt.
Serve hot with roti or fried rice.
notes: Jalfrezi is a quick stir-fry with bold flavors. Keep vegetables crunchy by cooking on high heat.

name: Veg Kadai
ingredients: 1.5 cups mixed vegetables (capsicum, onion, tomato, mushroom, peas)
preparation: Dry roast coriander seeds and dried red chillies. Grind coarsely for kadai masala.
Heat oil in a kadai or deep pan.
Add ginger-garlic paste and sauté briefly.
Add chopped onion and cook until soft.
Add tomatoes and cook until mushy.
Add the kadai masala, turmeric, and salt.
Cook masala for 3-4 minutes.
Add all vegetables and stir-fry for 5-7 minutes.
Add garam masala and crushed kasuri methi.
Garnish with coriander. Serve with tandoori roti.
notes: The freshly ground kadai masala of coriander and red chillies is the heart of this dish.

name: Bhindi Do Pyaza
preparation: Wash bhindi and dry thoroughly. Cut into 1-inch pieces.
Heat oil in a wide pan, add cumin seeds.
Add sliced onions and sauté until light golden.
Add bhindi and spread in a single layer. Cook on medium heat without stirring for 4-5 minutes.
Stir gently, add turmeric and salt.
Cook uncovered for 8-10 minutes until bhindi is crisp.
Add tomato, coriander powder, red chilli powder, and green chillies.
Cook for 3-4 minutes.
Add onion chunks (do pyaza), amchur, and garam masala.
Cook for 2 more minutes. Serve with roti.
notes: Do Pyaza adds raw onion chunks at the end for crunch contrast with the cooked bhindi.

name: Karela Sabzi
preparation: Slice karela thin, rub with salt and turmeric. Let sit for 30 minutes.
Squeeze out the bitter juice thoroughly. Rinse if desired.
Heat oil in a pan, add cumin seeds.
Add sliced onions and sauté until golden.
Add karela slices and spread in a single layer.
Cook on medium heat without stirring for 5 minutes.
Add coriander powder, red chilli powder, and salt.
Cook uncovered for 10-12 minutes, stirring occasionally, until crisp.
Add amchur and jaggery if using. Toss well.
Serve hot with roti and dal.
notes: Salting and squeezing removes bitterness. Karela is excellent for blood sugar management. Jaggery balances the bitterness.

name: Tinda Masala
ingredients: 6-8 tinda (Indian round gourd) peeled and halved
preparation: Peel tinda and cut into halves or quarters.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds.
Add onion and sauté until golden.
Add tomato, turmeric, coriander powder, and red chilli powder.
Cook until tomato becomes soft.
Add tinda pieces and salt. Mix well.
Add 1/4 cup water, cover and cook on low heat for 15-18 minutes.
Stir occasionally. Tinda should be tender when done.
Add amchur, garam masala, and green chillies.
Garnish with coriander and serve with roti.
notes: A simple North Indian sabzi. Tinda is very low in calories and easy to digest. Available in summer months.

name: Parwal Sabzi
preparation: Scrape parwal lightly and make a slit lengthwise.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds and fennel seeds.
Add chopped onion and cook until golden.
Add tomato, turmeric, coriander powder, and red chilli powder.
Cook until tomato is soft.
Add parwal, salt, and 2 tbsp water.
Cover and cook on low heat for 12-15 minutes.
Stir gently occasionally so parwal doesn't break.
Add garam masala when parwal is tender.
Garnish with coriander and serve with roti or dal rice.
notes: Parwal is a summer vegetable rich in vitamins. Scraping the skin makes it tender and allows masala to absorb.

name: Sem Ki Phali
ingredients: 250g sem (flat beans / valor) stringed and cut
preparation: String the flat beans and cut into 1-inch pieces.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds.
Add chopped onion and sauté until soft.
Add ginger-garlic paste and green chillies.
Add tomato, turmeric, coriander powder, and red chilli powder.
Cook until tomato is soft.
Add sem pieces, salt, and 1/4 cup water.
Cover and cook on low-medium heat for 15-18 minutes until tender.
Stir occasionally. Add garam masala.
Serve with roti or paratha.
notes: Sem ki phali is a seasonal winter vegetable. Cook covered on low heat for the best texture.

name: Aloo Matar
preparation: Cut potatoes into cubes. Shell fresh peas or thaw frozen ones.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds.
Add onion and sauté until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook briefly.
Add tomato, turmeric, coriander powder, and red chilli powder.
Cook until tomato softens.
Add potato cubes, salt, and 1/2 cup water.
Cover and cook for 8-10 minutes until potatoes are half done.
Add green peas and cook for another 5-7 minutes.
Add garam masala, garnish with coriander. Serve with roti or poori.
notes: A homestyle everyday curry loved by all. Use fresh peas in winter for the sweetest flavor.

name: Aloo Palak
preparation: Blanch spinach in hot water for 2 minutes, cool, and puree coarsely.
Cut potatoes into cubes.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds.
Add onion and sauté until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and green chillies.
Add tomato and all dry spices. Cook until soft.
Add potato cubes and 1/4 cup water. Cover and cook for 10 minutes.
Add spinach puree and mix well.
Cook for 5-7 more minutes until potatoes are fully done.
Add garam masala and serve with roti or rice.
notes: A nutritious combination of potatoes and spinach rich in iron and vitamin C.

name: Aloo Methi
preparation: Wash methi leaves thoroughly and chop. Remove tough stems.
Cut potatoes into small cubes.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds.
Add onion and cook until soft.
Add potato cubes, turmeric, and salt. Mix well.
Cover and cook for 8-10 minutes until potatoes are half done.
Add chopped methi leaves, coriander powder, red chilli powder, and green chillies.
Mix well, cover, and cook for another 5-7 minutes on low heat.
Add amchur powder and toss gently.
Serve hot with roti or paratha.
notes: Methi (fenugreek) has a slightly bitter taste that pairs beautifully with potatoes. Great for digestion.

name: Aloo Jeera
preparation: Boil potatoes until just tender. Peel and cut into cubes.
Heat oil in a pan, add cumin seeds.
Let them splutter and turn golden brown.
Add green chillies and cook briefly.
Add boiled potato cubes and turmeric.
Toss well and cook on medium heat for 5-7 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Add red chilli powder and salt.
Cook until potatoes get a light golden crust.
Add amchur and garam masala. Toss well.
Garnish with coriander and serve with poori or roti.
notes: The simplest and quickest potato dish. Cumin is the star here - use generously for best flavor.

name: Dum Aloo
preparation: Boil potatoes until just tender. Peel and prick with a fork.
Deep fry or pan-fry potatoes until golden. Set aside.
Heat oil, add bay leaf and cardamom.
Add onion puree and cook until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for a minute.
Add tomato puree, turmeric, red chilli powder, and coriander powder.
Cook until oil separates.
Add cashew paste and yogurt. Cook for 3-4 minutes.
Add 1 cup water and fried potatoes. Bring to a simmer.
Cover and cook on low heat (dum) for 10-12 minutes. Add garam masala. Serve with rice or naan.
notes: Dum means slow-cooked. Baby potatoes absorb the gravy better. A Kashmiri-Punjabi specialty.

name: Chicken Curry
ingredients: 500g chicken on bone cut into pieces
preparation: Wash and clean chicken pieces.
Heat oil in a heavy-bottomed pan. Add cumin seeds, bay leaves, cloves, cardamom, and cinnamon.
Add onions and sauté until deep golden brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 2 minutes.
Add tomato puree, turmeric, red chilli powder, and coriander powder.
Cook until oil separates from the masala (about 8-10 minutes).
Add chicken pieces and sear on high heat for 3-4 minutes.
Add 1 cup water, salt, and bring to a boil.
Cover and simmer on low heat for 25-30 minutes until chicken is cooked through.
Add garam masala, garnish with coriander. Serve with rice or roti.
notes: A classic Indian chicken curry. Cooking on bone gives better flavor. Adjust spices to taste.

name: Butter Chicken Light
ingredients: For marinade: 1/2 cup yogurt, 1 tsp red chilli powder, 1 tsp garam masala, 1 tbsp lemon juice
For gravy: 3 tomatoes pureed, 10-12 cashew nuts soaked, 1 tbsp ginger-garlic paste
preparation: Marinate chicken in yogurt, spices, and lemon juice for 1 hour.
Grill or pan-sear marinated chicken until lightly charred. Set aside.
Soak cashews and blend with tomatoes into a smooth puree.
Heat butter, add ginger-garlic paste and cook briefly.
Add the tomato-cashew puree, turmeric, and red chilli powder.
Cook for 10-12 minutes until sauce thickens.
Add sugar and salt.
Add the grilled chicken pieces to the gravy.
Add low-fat cream, garam masala, and crushed kasuri methi.
Simmer for 5 minutes. Serve with naan or rice.
notes: Lighter version uses less butter and low-fat cream. Grilling chicken first adds smoky flavor.

name: Chicken Tikka Masala
ingredients: For tikka marinade: 1/2 cup yogurt, 1 tsp red chilli powder, 1/2 tsp turmeric, 1 tsp garam masala, 1 tbsp lemon juice, 1 tsp ginger-garlic paste
For masala: 2 onions pureed, 2 tomatoes pureed, 1 tbsp ginger-garlic paste
preparation: Marinate chicken in yogurt and spices for at least 1 hour.
Thread onto skewers or spread on a baking tray. Grill at 220°C until charred edges.
For masala: Heat butter, add onion puree and cook until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for a minute.
Add tomato puree and cook until oil separates (10-12 minutes).
Add 1/2 cup water for desired consistency.
Add grilled chicken tikka pieces to the gravy.
Simmer for 5-7 minutes.
Add cream, garam masala, and kasuri methi.
Serve hot with naan or tandoori roti.
notes: The globally beloved Indian dish. Charring the tikka before adding to gravy is essential for authentic taste.

name: Chicken Korma Light
preparation: Soak cashews and blend to a smooth paste.
Fry sliced onions until golden brown, then blend to a paste.
Heat oil, add cardamom, cloves, bay leaf, and cinnamon.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for a minute.
Add onion paste and cook for 5 minutes until it turns deep brown.
Add yogurt, turmeric, and red chilli powder. Cook for 3 minutes.
Add chicken pieces and sear for 5 minutes.
Add cashew paste and 1/2 cup water.
Cover and cook for 20 minutes until chicken is tender.
Add cream, saffron, and garam masala. Serve with pulao or naan.
notes: A mild, creamy Mughlai curry. Light version uses less oil and low-fat cream while keeping the rich flavor.

name: Chicken Do Pyaza
preparation: Heat oil, add cumin seeds.
Add sliced onions and fry until deep golden brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 2 minutes.
Add tomatoes, turmeric, red chilli powder, and coriander powder.
Cook masala until oil separates.
Add yogurt and cook for 2-3 minutes.
Add chicken pieces and sear on high heat.
Add 1/2 cup water, salt, cover and cook for 25 minutes.
Add quartered onions (these should stay semi-crunchy).
Cook for 5 more minutes. Add garam masala and coriander. Serve with roti.
notes: Do pyaza means double onions - cooked for gravy base and raw chunks added at the end for texture.

name: Kadhi Pakora
ingredients: For kadhi: 1 cup yogurt (sour), 3 tbsp besan, 2 cups water
For pakoras: 1 cup besan, 1 onion chopped, 2 green chillies chopped, 1/4 tsp baking soda
For tadka: 1 tsp cumin seeds, 1 tsp mustard seeds, 8-10 curry leaves, 2 dried red chillies, 1/4 tsp asafoetida, 1/4 tsp fenugreek seeds, 1 tbsp ghee
preparation: For pakoras: Mix besan, onion, green chillies, baking soda, salt, and water into a thick batter.
Drop spoonfuls into hot oil and fry until golden. Drain and set aside.
For kadhi: Whisk yogurt and besan with water until smooth. No lumps.
Bring to a boil, stirring continuously to prevent curdling.
Add turmeric, red chilli powder, and salt. Simmer for 20-25 minutes.
Add the fried pakoras and simmer for 5 more minutes.
For tadka: Heat ghee, add all tadka ingredients.
Pour the sizzling tadka over the kadhi.
Stir well and serve hot with steamed rice.
notes: Use sour yogurt for authentic taste. Keep stirring while boiling to prevent curdling. A North Indian comfort food classic.

name: Rajasthani Kadhi
preparation: Whisk yogurt, besan, and water until smooth and lump-free.
Bring the mixture to a boil, stirring continuously.
Add turmeric, red chilli powder, and salt.
Simmer on low heat for 20-25 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Heat ghee for tadka. Add cumin seeds, mustard seeds, fenugreek seeds, and cloves.
Add dried red chillies, curry leaves, and asafoetida.
Pour the tadka into the simmering kadhi.
Cook for 5 more minutes.
Serve hot with steamed rice and papad.
notes: Rajasthani kadhi is thinner than Punjabi kadhi and doesn't have pakoras. Simple and wholesome.

name: Punjabi Kadhi
ingredients: For pakoras: 1/2 cup besan, 1 small onion chopped, pinch of baking soda
For tadka: 1 tsp cumin seeds, 1/4 tsp fenugreek seeds (methi dana)
preparation: Make small pakoras by mixing besan, onion, baking soda, salt, and water. Deep fry until golden.
Whisk yogurt with besan and water until smooth.
Bring to a boil while stirring continuously.
Add turmeric, red chilli powder, and salt. Simmer for 25-30 minutes.
Add fried pakoras and cook for 5 more minutes.
Heat ghee, add cumin seeds, fenugreek seeds, dried red chillies, curry leaves, and asafoetida.
Pour the hot tadka into the kadhi.
Stir well and simmer for 5 more minutes.
Serve steaming hot with jeera rice.
notes: The quintessential Punjabi kadhi-chawal. Fenugreek seeds in tadka are the signature Punjabi touch.

name: Gujarati Kadhi
preparation: Whisk yogurt, besan, and water until smooth.
Bring to a boil while stirring continuously.
Add salt and let it simmer on low heat for 15 minutes.
Add sugar or jaggery for the signature sweet-sour taste.
Heat oil, add mustard seeds and cumin seeds.
When they splutter, add cinnamon, cloves, curry leaves, green chillies, and asafoetida.
Pour the tadka into the kadhi.
Simmer for 10 more minutes.
Garnish with coriander and serve with khichdi or steamed rice.
notes: Gujarati kadhi has a distinctive sweet-sour-spicy taste. The addition of sugar or jaggery makes it unique.

name: Sindhi Kadhi
ingredients: 1 cup mixed vegetables (potato, drumstick, okra, cluster beans, tomato)
preparation: Mix besan with water and whisk until smooth.
Extract tamarind juice by soaking in warm water.
Boil the besan-water mixture while stirring. Simmer on low heat.
In another pan, heat oil. Add cumin, mustard, and fenugreek seeds.
Add curry leaves, dried red chillies, and onion. Cook until soft.
Add vegetables, turmeric, red chilli powder, and cook for 3-4 minutes.
Add the vegetables to the simmering besan liquid.
Add tamarind juice and salt.
Simmer for 20-25 minutes until vegetables are tender.
Serve hot with steamed rice.
notes: Sindhi kadhi is unique as it uses mixed vegetables and tamarind instead of yogurt. A tangy, hearty curry.

name: Fish Curry Kerala
ingredients: 500g fish (seer fish or pomfret) cut into steaks
2 tsp red chilli powder (Kashmiri for color)
preparation: Marinate fish pieces with turmeric and salt for 15 minutes.
Heat coconut oil, add mustard seeds.
When they splutter, add curry leaves and sliced onions.
Sauté until onions are soft.
Add ginger-garlic paste and green chillies.
Add tomatoes and cook until soft.
Add red chilli powder, remaining turmeric, and thin coconut milk.
Add kodampuli and bring to a gentle simmer.
Gently add fish pieces and cook on low heat for 10-12 minutes.
Add thick coconut milk and simmer 3 more minutes. Do not boil after adding thick coconut milk. Serve with rice.
notes: Kerala fish curry gets better the next day. Kodampuli (Malabar tamarind) gives it the authentic sour note.

name: Goan Fish Curry
preparation: Grind coconut, red chillies, coriander seeds, cumin seeds, peppercorns, and turmeric with a little water to a fine paste.
Extract tamarind juice.
Heat coconut oil, add chopped onion and sauté until soft.
Add green chillies and the ground coconut masala paste.
Cook for 5-7 minutes, stirring frequently.
Add 1.5 cups water and tamarind juice. Bring to a boil.
Add salt and simmer for 5 minutes.
Gently add fish pieces and cook on low heat for 10-12 minutes.
Do not stir - gently shake the pan if needed.
Serve hot with steamed rice.
notes: Authentic Goan fish curry uses coconut-based masala. Handle fish gently to prevent pieces from breaking.

name: Bengali Fish Curry
ingredients: Mustard oil for frying and cooking
preparation: Marinate fish with turmeric and salt for 15 minutes.
Heat mustard oil in a kadai until it smokes, then reduce heat.
Fry fish pieces until golden on both sides. Set aside.
In the same oil, add sliced onion and cook until soft.
Add ginger paste and cumin paste, sauté for a minute.
Add tomatoes, red chilli powder, and remaining turmeric.
Cook until tomatoes are soft and oil separates.
Add 1.5 cups water, salt, and sugar. Bring to a simmer.
Gently add the fried fish pieces.
Cook on low heat for 8-10 minutes. Add green chillies and coriander. Serve with rice.
notes: Bengali fish curry must be cooked in mustard oil for authentic flavor. Frying the fish first is a Bengali tradition.

name: Malabar Fish Curry
preparation: Rub fish with turmeric and salt. Set aside for 15 minutes.
Heat coconut oil, add mustard seeds and fenugreek seeds.
When they splutter, add curry leaves and sliced onions.
Cook until onions turn soft.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook briefly.
Add tomatoes, red chilli powder, and remaining turmeric. Cook until soft.
Add thin coconut milk and bring to a gentle boil.
Add green chillies and simmer for 5 minutes.
Gently place fish pieces in the gravy.
Cook on low heat for 12 minutes. Add thick coconut milk and simmer 2-3 minutes. Serve with appam or rice.
notes: Malabar curry is coconut-rich and mildly spiced. Adding thick coconut milk at the end keeps the gravy creamy.

name: Chettinad Fish Curry
preparation: Dry roast fennel, peppercorns, red chillies, coriander seeds, cumin seeds, cinnamon, and star anise. Grind to a fine powder.
Marinate fish with turmeric and salt.
Heat oil, add curry leaves and onions. Sauté until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for a minute.
Add tomatoes and cook until mushy.
Add the ground Chettinad spice powder and cook for 3-4 minutes.
Add 1.5 cups water and tamarind extract.
Bring to a simmer, add salt.
Gently add fish pieces and cook for 12-15 minutes on low heat.
Serve hot with steamed rice.
notes: Chettinad cuisine from Tamil Nadu is known for bold spices. Star anise and fennel give this curry its unique aroma.

name: Egg Curry
preparation: Boil eggs for 10 minutes, peel and make 2-3 slits on each.
Optionally, lightly fry the boiled eggs until golden. Set aside.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds.
Add onions and sauté until golden brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for a minute.
Add tomato puree, turmeric, red chilli powder, and coriander powder.
Cook until oil separates from the masala.
Add 1 cup water and bring to a simmer.
Add the eggs and green chillies.
Simmer for 8-10 minutes. Add garam masala and coriander. Serve with rice or roti.
notes: A budget-friendly protein-rich curry. Frying the eggs is optional but gives a nice golden crust.

name: Egg Masala
preparation: Hard boil eggs, peel, and make slits.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds and curry leaves.
Add onion puree and cook until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and sauté for a minute.
Add tomato puree, turmeric, red chilli powder, and coriander powder.
Cook until masala thickens and oil separates.
Add coconut milk and 1/2 cup water.
Bring to a simmer. Add salt.
Add boiled eggs and simmer for 8-10 minutes.
Add garam masala, garnish with coriander. Serve with rice or parotta.
notes: South Indian-style egg masala with coconut milk. Richer and creamier than regular egg curry.

name: Anda Bhurji Gravy
preparation: Heat butter, add cumin seeds.
Add chopped onion and cook until golden.
Add green chillies and cook briefly.
Add tomato, turmeric, red chilli powder, and pav bhaji masala.
Cook until tomato is soft and mushy.
Add 1/2 cup water to create a gravy base.
Crack eggs directly into the gravy.
Stir and scramble the eggs in the gravy.
Cook for 3-4 minutes until eggs are done.
Add garam masala and coriander. Serve with pav, roti, or rice.
notes: Mumbai street-style anda bhurji with a gravy twist. Pav bhaji masala adds the signature street food flavor.

name: Egg Biryani
preparation: Soak rice and parboil with whole spices until 70% done. Drain.
Hard boil eggs, peel, and make slits. Lightly fry until golden.
Heat oil, fry onions until crispy golden. Set aside.
In the same oil, add ginger-garlic paste.
Add tomatoes, biryani masala, turmeric, red chilli powder, and yogurt.
Cook masala for 5-7 minutes.
Layer in a heavy pot: masala, fried eggs, parboiled rice.
Top with saffron milk, fried onions, mint, and coriander.
Seal and cook on dum (very low heat) for 20 minutes.
Serve with raita and salan.
notes: A budget-friendly biryani option. Frying eggs before layering gives a beautiful golden color.

name: Nargisi Kofta Light
ingredients: For gravy: 2 onions pureed, 2 tomatoes pureed
preparation: Season raw keema with ginger-garlic paste, garam masala, chopped onion, and salt.
Wrap each boiled egg with a layer of seasoned keema.
Pan-fry the wrapped eggs on medium heat until keema is cooked and golden on all sides.
For gravy: Heat oil, add onion puree and cook until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste, tomato puree, and all spices. Cook until oil separates.
Add yogurt and 1 cup water. Simmer for 10 minutes.
Cut the koftas in half and arrange in the gravy.
Simmer for 5 minutes.
Garnish with coriander. Serve with naan or pulao.
notes: A Mughlai delicacy - eggs wrapped in spiced mince. Pan-frying instead of deep frying makes it lighter.

name: Keema Matar
preparation: Heat oil, add cumin seeds.
Add onions and sauté until deep golden brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 2 minutes.
Add keema and cook on high heat, breaking up lumps, for 5-7 minutes.
Add tomatoes, turmeric, red chilli powder, and coriander powder.
Cook until tomatoes are soft and oil separates.
Add green peas and 1/2 cup water.
Cover and cook for 15-20 minutes until keema is fully cooked.
Add green chillies, garam masala, and coriander.
Serve with roti, paratha, or rice.
notes: A beloved North Indian mince dish with sweet peas. Cook keema on high heat initially to get a nice brown color.

name: Keema Aloo
preparation: Heat oil, add cumin seeds.
Add onions and cook until golden brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste and sauté for 2 minutes.
Add keema and cook on high heat until browned, about 5 minutes.
Add tomatoes, turmeric, red chilli powder, and coriander powder.
Cook until tomatoes break down.
Add potato cubes and 1 cup water.
Cover and cook for 20-25 minutes until potatoes and keema are done.
Add green chillies and garam masala.
Garnish with coriander. Serve with roti or paratha.
notes: Potato cubes absorb the keema gravy flavors beautifully. A hearty and filling lunch option.

name: Mutton Rogan Josh Light
ingredients: 2 tsp Kashmiri red chilli powder (for color)
preparation: Wash mutton pieces well.
Heat oil, add bay leaves, cloves, black cardamom, and cinnamon.
Add onion puree and cook until deep brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste and sauté for 2 minutes.
Add yogurt and cook for 3-4 minutes.
Add Kashmiri red chilli powder, turmeric, coriander powder, and fennel powder.
Cook the masala until oil separates.
Add mutton pieces and sear on high heat for 5 minutes.
Add 2 cups water, salt, and bring to a boil.
Cover and cook on very low heat for 1.5-2 hours until mutton is tender. Add ginger powder. Serve with rice or naan.
notes: A Kashmiri masterpiece. Light version uses less oil. Kashmiri chilli gives vibrant red color without too much heat.

name: Lamb Curry Light
preparation: Heat oil, add cumin seeds, bay leaves, cloves, cardamom, and cinnamon.
Add onions and cook until deep golden brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste and sauté for 2 minutes.
Add yogurt and cook for 3 minutes.
Add tomato puree, turmeric, red chilli powder, and coriander powder.
Cook until masala thickens and oil separates.
Add lamb pieces and sear on high heat for 5 minutes.
Add 2 cups water and salt. Bring to a boil.
Cover and simmer for 1-1.5 hours until lamb is tender.
Add garam masala and coriander. Serve with rice or naan.
notes: Slow cooking on low heat makes the lamb incredibly tender. Light version uses minimal oil.

name: Gosht Masala Light
preparation: Heat oil, add cumin seeds and whole spices.
Add sliced onions and fry until dark golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 2 minutes.
Add yogurt gradually, stirring well.
Add turmeric, red chilli powder, and coriander powder.
Cook masala until oil separates.
Add mutton pieces and sear on high heat for 5 minutes.
Add tomato puree and cook for 5 more minutes.
Add 2 cups water and salt. Bring to a boil.
Cover and slow cook for 1.5-2 hours until meat falls off the bone.
Add garam masala, mint, and coriander. Serve with naan or rice.
notes: Gosht masala needs patience - slow cooking is key. Black cardamom adds the distinctive smoky Mughlai aroma.

name: Aloo Tamatar Ki Sabzi
preparation: Cut potatoes into cubes.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds and asafoetida.
Add green chillies and potato cubes.
Add turmeric and stir well. Cook for 5 minutes.
Add chopped tomatoes, coriander powder, and red chilli powder.
Add salt and 1/4 cup water.
Cover and cook on low heat for 15-18 minutes until potatoes are tender.
Tomatoes should form a thick gravy.
Add amchur and garam masala.
Garnish with coriander. Serve with poori or roti.
notes: A quick no-onion-no-garlic sabzi. Tomatoes provide the tangy gravy. Perfect with hot pooris.

name: Lauki Chana Dal
ingredients: 1 medium lauki (bottle gourd) peeled and cubed
preparation: Soak chana dal for 30 minutes.
Peel and cube the lauki.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds and asafoetida.
Add onion and sauté until golden.
Add tomato, turmeric, red chilli powder, and coriander powder.
Cook until tomato is soft.
Add soaked chana dal and 1.5 cups water.
Pressure cook for 2 whistles.
Open, add lauki cubes, green chillies, and salt.
Cook on medium heat for 10-15 minutes until lauki is tender. Garnish with coriander. Serve with roti.
notes: A light and digestible combination. Lauki is 90% water and very cooling. Great for weight watchers.

name: Turai Ki Sabzi
preparation: Peel turai (remove the ridges) and slice into rounds.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds.
Add onion and sauté until soft.
Add tomato, turmeric, red chilli powder, and coriander powder.
Cook until tomato softens.
Add turai slices, green chillies, and salt.
Cover and cook on low heat for 10-12 minutes.
Turai will release water - no need to add extra water.
Add garam masala when turai is tender.
Serve with roti or dal rice.
notes: Turai (ridge gourd) is very low calorie and easy to digest. A simple summer sabzi that cooks in minutes.

name: Arbi Masala
preparation: Boil arbi until just tender. Peel and cut into halves or quarters.
Heat oil in a wide pan, add cumin seeds.
Add arbi pieces in a single layer and pan-fry until golden and crisp on all sides.
Add chopped onion around the arbi and cook until soft.
Add turmeric, coriander powder, red chilli powder, and salt.
Toss gently to coat arbi with spices.
Add green chillies and cook for 3-4 minutes.
Add amchur and garam masala.
Garnish with coriander and serve with roti or poori.
notes: Pan-frying boiled arbi gives a crispy exterior. Use a non-stick pan and handle gently as arbi can be sticky.

name: Kathal Ki Sabzi
preparation: Oil your hands and knife before cutting raw jackfruit (to prevent sticky sap).
Cut jackfruit into small pieces and boil until just tender. Drain.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds.
Add onion and sauté until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook briefly.
Add tomato, turmeric, coriander powder, and red chilli powder.
Cook until tomato is soft.
Add boiled jackfruit pieces and salt.
Cover and cook on low heat for 10-12 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Add garam masala and coriander. Serve with roti.
notes: Raw jackfruit has a meat-like texture and is called 'vegetarian meat'. Oil hands and knife well before cutting to avoid stickiness.

name: Chana Masala Dry
preparation: Soak chickpeas overnight and pressure cook until soft but not mushy.
Drain the chickpeas, reserving some liquid.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds.
Add onion and cook until golden.
Add tomato, turmeric, red chilli powder, and coriander powder.
Cook until tomato is soft.
Add cooked chickpeas and mix well.
Cook on medium heat for 5-7 minutes to dry out excess moisture.
Add amchur, chaat masala, garam masala, and green chillies.
Garnish with coriander and lemon. Serve as a side with roti or in a wrap.
notes: Dry version of chole masala - great as a side dish or for stuffing in wraps and sandwiches.

name: Sev Tamatar
preparation: Heat oil, add cumin seeds and asafoetida.
Add onion and sauté until soft.
Add tomatoes, turmeric, red chilli powder, and coriander powder.
Cook until tomatoes become soft and form a thick gravy.
Add green chillies, sugar, and salt.
Simmer for 5 minutes.
Just before serving, add sev to the tomato gravy and mix.
The sev will absorb the gravy and soften.
Garnish with coriander and more sev on top.
Serve immediately with roti. Do not let it sit long or sev gets too soft.
notes: A Rajasthani quickie! Add sev just before serving for the best texture. Sugar balances the tomato sourness.

name: Besan Gatta Curry
ingredients: For gattas: 1 cup besan, 1/4 tsp turmeric, 1/2 tsp red chilli powder, 1/4 tsp ajwain (carom seeds), 1 tbsp oil, salt, water
For curry: 1/2 cup yogurt, 2 tbsp besan, 1 onion chopped, 1 tsp cumin seeds, 1/2 tsp turmeric, 1/2 tsp red chilli powder, 1 tsp coriander powder
preparation: Mix besan with turmeric, red chilli powder, ajwain, oil, salt, and enough water to make a firm dough.
Roll into long cylindrical logs (like thick fingers).
Boil in water for 10-12 minutes. Remove and cut into 1-inch pieces.
For curry: Whisk yogurt with 2 tbsp besan and 1 cup water until smooth.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds and onion. Sauté until golden.
Add turmeric, red chilli, and coriander powder.
Add the yogurt-besan mixture slowly, stirring continuously.
Bring to a simmer and cook for 10 minutes.
Add the boiled gatta pieces. Simmer for 10 more minutes.
Add garam masala and coriander. Serve with rice or roti.
notes: A classic Rajasthani dish - besan dumplings in spiced yogurt gravy. No fresh vegetables needed, perfect for arid regions.

name: Papad Ki Sabzi
preparation: Break papads into medium-sized pieces (do not fry).
Whisk yogurt with besan and water until smooth.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds and asafoetida.
Add onion and sauté until golden.
Add tomato, turmeric, red chilli powder, and coriander powder.
Cook until tomato is soft.
Add the yogurt-besan mixture slowly, stirring constantly.
Bring to a simmer and cook for 8-10 minutes.
Add papad pieces and stir gently.
Cook for 3-4 minutes. Papad will soften. Garnish with coriander. Serve with rice.
notes: A clever Rajasthani recipe using pantry staples. Makes a quick curry when no vegetables are available.

name: Ker Sangri
preparation: Soak dried ker and sangri in water overnight.
Boil them until tender. Drain well.
Heat oil in a pan, add cumin seeds and asafoetida.
Add dried red chillies.
Add the boiled ker and sangri.
Add turmeric, red chilli powder, coriander powder, and salt.
Mix well and cook on medium heat for 10-12 minutes.
Stir occasionally to prevent sticking.
Add amchur powder and toss well.
Serve with bajra roti and ghee.
notes: A signature Rajasthani dish from the Thar desert. These dried desert berries and beans are nutritious and store well.

name: Methi Malai Matar
preparation: Wash and chop methi leaves. Remove tough stems.
Soak cashews and blend to a smooth paste.
Heat butter, add cumin seeds.
Add onion and sauté until soft.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook briefly.
Add methi leaves and sauté for 4-5 minutes until wilted.
Add green peas, turmeric, red chilli powder, and salt.
Cook for 5 minutes.
Add cashew paste and 1/2 cup water. Simmer for 5 minutes.
Add cream and garam masala. Cook 2 more minutes. Serve with naan or paratha.
notes: A creamy, mildly bitter curry from North India. The cream tames the bitterness of methi leaves beautifully.

name: Mushroom Matar
preparation: Wash and slice mushrooms. Blanch peas if using fresh.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds.
Add onion and sauté until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for a minute.
Add tomato puree, turmeric, coriander powder, and red chilli powder.
Cook until oil separates from masala.
Add mushrooms and sauté for 3-4 minutes until they release water.
Add green peas and 1/4 cup water.
Cook for 5-7 minutes until mushrooms are done.
Add garam masala and coriander. Serve with roti or rice.
notes: A light, protein-rich curry. Mushrooms release water while cooking, so don't add too much water initially.

name: Mushroom Masala
preparation: Wash and quarter the mushrooms.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds.
Add onions and cook until deep golden brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook for 2 minutes.
Add tomato puree, turmeric, red chilli powder, and coriander powder.
Cook until masala thickens and oil separates.
Add 1/4 cup water for gravy consistency.
Add mushroom quarters and salt.
Cook for 8-10 minutes until mushrooms are tender.
Add garam masala, kasuri methi, and cream if using. Serve with naan or roti.
notes: Restaurant-style mushroom masala with rich gravy. Mushrooms are low-calorie and high in B vitamins.

name: Baby Corn Masala
preparation: Cut baby corn into 1-inch pieces.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds.
Add onion and sauté until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and cook briefly.
Add tomato puree, turmeric, coriander powder, and red chilli powder.
Cook until masala thickens.
Add baby corn pieces and capsicum.
Add 1/4 cup water and salt. Cook for 8-10 minutes.
Baby corn should be tender but still have a slight crunch.
Add garam masala and coriander. Serve with roti or fried rice.
notes: Baby corn is low in calories and adds great crunch. Don't overcook - it should remain slightly crisp.

name: Corn Palak
preparation: Blanch spinach in hot water for 2 minutes. Blend to a smooth puree.
Boil corn kernels until tender.
Heat butter, add cumin seeds.
Add onion and cook until soft.
Add ginger-garlic paste and green chillies.
Add tomato and cook until soft.
Add spinach puree and turmeric. Cook for 5 minutes.
Add corn kernels, salt, and cook for 3-4 minutes.
Add cream if using and garam masala.
Serve with roti or naan.
notes: Sweet corn and spinach make a nutritious, colorful combination. Kids love this dish for its mild sweetness.

name: Sambar Rice
ingredients: 1/2 cup mixed vegetables (drumstick, carrot, brinjal, pumpkin)
preparation: Cook rice and toor dal separately.
Extract tamarind juice. Boil vegetables until tender.
Heat oil, add mustard seeds. When they splutter, add curry leaves and asafoetida.
Add onion and cook until soft.
Add tomato and cook until mushy.
Add sambhar powder and turmeric. Cook for 2 minutes.
Add cooked vegetables, tamarind juice, and mashed dal.
Simmer for 10-12 minutes.
Mix the sambhar with rice or serve alongside.
Garnish with coriander. Serve with papad and pickle.
notes: A complete South Indian meal. Mixing sambhar with rice is the traditional way to eat it.

name: Rasam Rice
ingredients: For rasam: 1/4 cup toor dal cooked
preparation: Cook toor dal and mash well. Extract tamarind juice.
Boil tomatoes with tamarind juice, turmeric, and rasam powder.
Add mashed dal and 2 cups water. Bring to a boil.
Let it simmer for 8-10 minutes. Add salt.
Heat ghee, add mustard seeds and cumin seeds.
When they splutter, add curry leaves, dried red chillies, garlic, and asafoetida.
Pour this tadka into the rasam.
Add coriander leaves.
Serve hot rasam poured over steamed rice.
Pair with papad and a side of pickle.
notes: Rasam rice is a South Indian comfort food. The tangy, peppery rasam aids digestion and is soothing when unwell.

name: Kootu
ingredients: 1 cup chana dal or moong dal soaked
1 cup mixed vegetables (raw banana, yam, pumpkin)
preparation: Soak dal for 30 minutes. Cook until half done.
Cook vegetables separately until tender.
Grind coconut with cumin seeds, peppercorns, and red chillies to a paste.
Combine cooked dal and vegetables in a pan.
Add turmeric, salt, and the coconut paste.
Simmer on low heat for 10 minutes, stirring gently.
Heat coconut oil, add mustard seeds, curry leaves, and asafoetida.
Pour the tadka over the kootu.
Stir gently and serve with rice.
notes: A Tamil Nadu staple - lentils and vegetables in a coconut-spice paste. Part of traditional thali meals.

name: Avial
ingredients: 1 cup mixed vegetables (drumstick, raw banana, yam, carrot, beans, ash gourd)
preparation: Cut all vegetables into uniform finger-length pieces.
Cook vegetables with turmeric and salt until just tender (not mushy).
Grind coconut, cumin seeds, and green chillies to a coarse paste.
Add the coconut paste to the cooked vegetables.
Mix gently and simmer for 5 minutes on low heat.
Remove from heat and add whisked yogurt.
Stir gently without boiling (to prevent yogurt from curdling).
Drizzle coconut oil and add curry leaves.
Let it rest for 5 minutes for flavors to meld.
Serve with rice as part of a Kerala sadya or any meal.
notes: A Kerala festival dish featuring mixed vegetables in coconut-yogurt. Use at least 5-6 different vegetables for authentic avial.

name: Olan
ingredients: 1/2 cup black-eyed peas (lobhia) soaked and cooked
preparation: Soak and cook black-eyed peas until tender.
Cut ash gourd into cubes.
Cook ash gourd in thin coconut milk with green chillies and salt.
Cook until ash gourd is just tender (about 10 minutes).
Add cooked black-eyed peas.
Simmer for 5 minutes.
Add thick coconut milk and curry leaves.
Simmer for 2-3 minutes. Do not boil after adding thick coconut milk.
Drizzle coconut oil on top.
Serve as part of a Kerala sadya with rice.
notes: A mild, coconut-milk based Kerala dish with no spice powders. The simplicity is its beauty.

name: Thoran
preparation: Finely shred the vegetable (cabbage, beans, or beetroot).
Heat coconut oil, add mustard seeds and urad dal.
When they splutter, add curry leaves and onion.
Cook until onion is soft.
Add the shredded vegetable, turmeric, green chillies, and salt.
Mix well, cover and cook on low heat for 8-10 minutes.
The vegetable should be cooked but retain some crunch.
Add grated coconut and toss well.
Cook for 2-3 more minutes uncovered.
Serve as a side dish with rice and sambhar.
notes: A Kerala dry vegetable dish with coconut. Essential part of Kerala meals. Can be made with any vegetable.

name: Mezhukkupuratti
ingredients: 250g raw banana or yam or okra sliced
preparation: Slice the vegetable (raw banana, yam, or okra) into thin rounds.
Cook the vegetable with turmeric, salt, and a little water until just tender.
Drain any excess water.
Heat coconut oil in a pan.
Add dried red chillies and curry leaves.
Add sliced shallots and cook until golden.
Add the cooked vegetable and red chilli powder.
Stir-fry on medium heat for 5-7 minutes until slightly crisp.
Toss well until oil coats every piece.
Serve as a side with rice and rasam or sambhar.
notes: Kerala-style stir-fried vegetable in coconut oil. The name literally means 'stirred in oil'. Minimal spices, maximum flavor.

name: Kalan
preparation: Cook raw banana and yam cubes with turmeric and salt until tender.
Grind coconut with cumin seeds and green chillies to a smooth paste.
Add the coconut paste to the cooked vegetables.
Simmer on low heat for 5 minutes.
Beat the sour yogurt well and add to the curry.
Cook on very low heat for 5 minutes - do not boil.
Heat coconut oil, add mustard seeds and curry leaves.
Pour the tadka over the kalan.
Let it rest for a few minutes before serving.
Serve with rice as part of a traditional Kerala sadya.
notes: A tangy, thick Kerala curry using sour yogurt and coconut. Essential dish in the Onam sadya.

name: Pachadi
ingredients: 1 cup cucumber or pineapple or ash gourd grated
preparation: Cook the grated vegetable or fruit with turmeric and salt until soft.
Grind coconut with green chillies to a paste.
Add the coconut paste to the cooked vegetable.
Cook for 3-4 minutes on low heat.
Remove from heat and let it cool slightly.
Add whisked yogurt and mix gently.
Heat coconut oil, add mustard seeds, dried red chilli, and curry leaves.
